standard_description: Use modal auxiliaries (e.g., can, may, must) to convey various conditions.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Perfect progressive verb aspect
khan_exercises.description: If an action has been ongoing for some time, or was once ongoing and has since completed, we say it's in the progressive perfect aspect! Test your knowledge of this grammatical aspect.

items.question_content:
**Choose the [[☃ definition 1]] form of the verb!**

>They _\_\_\_\_\_\_ _ for ages.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Verb aspect** allows you to be more specific about when an action occurs:

* **Progressive aspect** shows that an action is *ongoing* using a form of "to be" alongside an -ing verb.
* **Perfect aspect** allows us to talk about a completed action using a form of "to have" alongside a past tense verb.

If we combine these aspects, we get the **progressive perfect** aspect, which shows that something is both *ongoing* and either *completed in the past* or *continuing in the present*.

To create a **progressive perfect** verb phrase, we use a form of "to have" *and* a form of "to be" alongside an -ing verb.
The progressive perfect form is **"have been chatting"**.

options.definition: The *progressive perfect* aspect shows that actions started in the past and are still ongoing. Look for "-ing" verbs (that's the progressive part) and the words *have*, *has*, or *had* (that's the perfect part).

options.definition: The *progressive perfect* aspect or tense shows actions or events that started in the past and are still ongoing. Look for "-ing" verbs (that's the progressive part) and the words *have*, *has*, or *had* (that's the perfect part).

khan_exercises.title: Managing time with tense and aspect
khan_exercises.description: Using what you've already learned about tense and aspect, use your time wizardry to make sense of these sentences.
topic_path: Managing time with tense and aspect

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Yesterday, I \_\_\_\_\_\_ breakfast at 6:30.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that happened "yesterday", so we should use a **past tense** verb.
Of the choices, only **"ate"** is a past tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Next summer, Erin \_\_\_\_\_\_ monkeys in the forests of West Africa.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that will happen "next summer", so we should use the *future tense*.
Of the choices, only **"will study"** is a future tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Courtney \_\_\_\_\_\_ to Rochester before.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that has never happened until the present ("before"), so we should use the *present perfect tense*.
Of the choices, only **"has never been"** is a present perfect tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Mark \_\_\_\_\_\_ when the doorbell rang.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that was ongoing in the *past*, so we should use the *past progressive tense*.
Of the choices, only **"was sleeping"** is a past progressive verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Simon \_\_\_\_\_\_ the crime scene carefully before he began taking pictures.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that happened in the *past*, so we should use the *past tense*.
Of the choices, only **"examined"** is a past tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Unfortunately, we are \_\_\_\_\_\_ from going in the Cave of Flames.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence uses "prohibit" as an *adjective*, so we should use the *past participle* form.
Of the choices, only **"prohibited"** is a past participle.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>We had been \_\_\_\_\_\_ that there would be no bats in here!  I’m allergic to bats!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence uses "had been" as to set up the blank, so we should use the *past participle* form of "assure".
Of the choices, only **"assured"** is a past participle.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Mayra was so \_\_\_\_\_\_ in her work that she didn’t notice that the lights had gone off.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence uses "engage" as an *adjective*, so we should use the *past participle* form.
Of the choices, only **"engaged"** is a past participle.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Wanda \_\_\_\_\_\_ a technical diagram in her studio when the clock struck midnight.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that was ongoing in the *past*, so we should use the *past progressive tense*.
Of the choices, only **"was illustrating"** is a past progressive verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Jeremy is \_\_\_\_\_\_ me to apply for the job.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that is ongoing in the *present*, so we should use the *progressive tense*.
Of the choices, only **"urging"** creates a progressive verb alongside "is".

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>In the future, I hope we \_\_\_\_\_\_ with aliens!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that will happen "in the future", so we should use the *future tense*.
Of the choices, only **"will trade"** is a future tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>James \_\_\_\_\_\_ against his parents when he was a kid by wearing mismatched socks.  They didn’t notice.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that happened in the *past* ("when he was a kid"), so we should use the *past tense*.
Of the choices, only **"rebelled"** is a past tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Dr. Kramer will \_\_\_\_\_\_ the X-ray and project it on that screen in front of you.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that will happen in the *future*, so we should use the *future tense*.
Of the choices, only **"magnify"** creates a future tense verb alongside "will".

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Danielle \_\_\_\_\_\_ the vase carefully, but she couldn’t find any fractures.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that happened in the *past*, so we should use the *past tense*.
Of the choices, only **"inspected"** is a past tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>In a few days from now, the mold \_\_\_\_\_\_ the entire corridor.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that will be completed in the *future* ("a few days from now"), so we should use the *future perfect tense*.
Of the choices, only **"will have infected"** is a future perfect verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Each morning, Fred \_\_\_\_\_\_ the tarp from his car.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that happens every day, so we should use the *present tense*.
Of the choices, only **"removes"** is a present tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>The rope is \_\_\_\_\_\_ too quickly!  You’re going to have to jump!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that is ongoing in the *present*, so we should use the *progressive tense*.
Of the choices, only **"fraying"** creates a progressive verb alongside "is".

items.question_content:
**Choose the form of the verb that best matches the time frame of the sentence.**

>Ira \_\_\_\_\_\_ with lightning speed and caught the kitten as it fell from the tree.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This sentence describes something that happened in the *past*, so we should use the *past tense*.
Of the choices, only **"reacted"** is a past tense verb, so it's the best verb to use in this sentence.

khan_exercises.title: Modal verbs
khan_exercises.description: The modal verbs of English are really powerful; you can use them to ask for permission, give advice, determine how likely something is, and many other things besides! Test your knowledge of the modals!

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>Didier **can** swim, but he’s not wild about the ocean.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In the sentence, Didier isn't asking anyone for permission. So in this instance, ***can* shows Didier's ability to swim.**

choices.content: Didier is using _can_ to ask for  **permission** to swim.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>I **must** go to sleep by 9 if I want to wake up at 4.  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
"I *must* go to sleep" means that I *need* to go to sleep. So in this instance, ***must* means that something is necessary.**

choices.content: This sentence uses _must_ to **ask for permission**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>When she was little, Sarah **could** stand on her head, but she can’t anymore.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In the sentence, Sarah isn't asking anyone for permission. So in this instance, ***could* shows Sarah's ability to stand on her head.**

choices.content: _Could_ is used to indicate that Sarah **wants permission** to stand on her head.
_Could_ shows that Sarah **had the ability** to stand on her head.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>“The thieves **must** have gone this way!” Detective Ramona cried.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In the sentence, Ramona isn't giving anyone an order. So in this instance, ***must* is used to make a confident claim.**

choices.content: Detective Ramona is using _must_ to **make a confident claim about whether or not something is true**.
Detective Ramona is using _must_ to **give someone else an order**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>“**May** I keep my shoes on?” Jess asked.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
**Jess is using *may* to ask for permission.**

choices.content: Jess is using _may_ to **ask for permission.**
_May_ is being used to **express how likely something is**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>“Keep your seatbelts on.  We **may** run into some rough weather,” said the pilot.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
**The pilot is using *may* to suggest that rough weather is a possibility.**

choices.content: The pilot is using _may_ to talk about the **possibility** of rough weather.
The pilot is using _may_ to **give someone permission**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>“I **shall** have lunch in my treehouse today,” Elliott said.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
**Elliot is using *shall* to talk about the future.**

choices.content: Elliott is using _shall_ to **give an order**.
Elliott is using _shall_ to **talk about the future**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>You **can** learn anything.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
**In this sentence, *can* means that you have the *ability* to learn anything.**

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>We **couldn’t** go to the zoo, because it was closed.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, *couldn't* means that **it was not possible.**

choices.content: _Couldn't_ is being used here to express **that something wasn't possible**.
This sentence uses _couldn't_ to **request permission** to go to the zoo.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>It looks like it **could** rain today.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, *could* means that rain seems **likely or possible**.

choices.content: This sentence uses _could_ to **ask for permission**.
This sentence uses _could_ to **express how likely it is** that it will rain.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>**Could** we visit Noah when we go to Cedar Rapids?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, *could* is used to **ask for permission.**

choices.content: The speaker is using _could_ to **talk about their ability** to visit Noah.
The speaker is using _could_ to **ask for permission** to visit Noah.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>"**Would** an icepack help?" Cesar asked.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, Cesar uses *would* to **express uncertainty** and ask a question.

choices.content: Cesar is using *would* to **express uncertainty **about whether an icepack will help.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>**Can** we have dessert before dinner today?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, *can* is being used to **ask for permission.**

choices.content: The sentence uses _can_ to talk about **the future**.
_Can_ is being used here to **ask for permission**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>You **shouldn’t** drink orange juice after brushing your teeth.  It tastes awful.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, *shouldn't* is used to **give advice.**

choices.content: _Shouldn't_ is being used here to **ask for permission**.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>I checked the forecast, and the sun **should** come out by noon.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this sentence, *should* is used to express that something is **probable.**

choices.content: _Should_ is being used in this sentence to give **advice**.
This sentence uses _should_ to express that the sun will **probably** come out.

items.question_content:
In the sentence below, what is the **bold** modal verb being used to do?

>Next week, Elizabeth **will** go to Japan!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Modal verbs are “helpers” that allow us to show likelihood, ability, need, or permission. The modal verbs in English are _may, might, must, can, could, shall, should, will,_ and _would._
In this instance, *will* is used to talk about **the future** ("next week").

choices.content: _Will_ indicates that Elizabeth is going to Japan in **the future**.
_Will_ is used in this sentence to ask a **question**.
